title: 18_payload_shape_not_status.jsx
descriptionMd:
## Mission: 200 OK but the Payload Says No

The checkout component calls `/api/coupons/apply`. The API replies with HTTP **200 OK** and a body like `{ "ok": false, "error": "COUPON_EXPIRED" }`. Your code checks `res.ok` (which is `true` because the status is 200), parses the JSON, and tries to read `data.discount.percent` to render the savings. Boom — `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'percent')` — because there's no `discount` field on an error payload.

Many APIs follow this pattern: **HTTP status is reserved for transport-level outcomes** (the request reached the server, the server understood it), while **business-logic errors come through the body** (`{ ok: false, error: ... }`). Your frontend has to check **both layers** — status AND payload shape. Status-only checks miss semantic errors; payload-only checks miss server failures. Add the payload check.
traceback:
User enters expired code, clicks Apply.
Server responds: HTTP 200 OK { "ok": false, "error": "COUPON_EXPIRED" }
res.ok is true (status is 200) — first check passes.
await res.json() returns { ok: false, error: "COUPON_EXPIRED" }
data.discount is undefined; data.discount.percent throws:

  TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'percent')
    at applyCoupon (coupon-input.jsx:24)

The try/catch we didn't write means the error becomes an unhandled rejection.
Button becomes unresponsive; user has no idea what went wrong.
correctOutput:
User enters expired code, clicks Apply. Server responds: HTTP 200 OK { ok: false, error: "COUPON_EXPIRED" } res.ok passes; data is parsed. We now also check data.ok — false, so we set the error state with the server's error string. The UI shows:

COUPON_EXPIRED

No crash, no unhandled rejection. The user sees a meaningful message and can retry with a different code.

options.label: Check `data.ok` after parsing: if false, surface the server's error; only read `data.discount` when `data.ok === true`
options.resultLog:
Expired-code case:
  Server: 200 { ok: false, error: "COUPON_EXPIRED" }
  Component: shows "COUPON_EXPIRED" in the error region. Button is responsive.

Valid-code case:
  Server: 200 { ok: true, discount: { percent: 15 } }
  Component: shows "15% off applied!" in the success region.

Both code paths are explicit; both states have a visible UI.
options.rationale: **Status and payload are two different layers.** HTTP status is reserved for transport-level outcomes (did the request reach the server? did the server understand it?). The body carries **business-logic outcomes** — was the coupon valid, did the user have permission, did the operation succeed semantically. APIs that follow this pattern (Stripe, GraphQL-over-HTTP, most modern REST APIs) need a two-layer check: status first (filters out 4xx/5xx), then payload (filters out semantic rejections). The `data.ok` boolean is the contract — only read business fields like `data.discount` after confirming `data.ok === true`. The `data.error ?? "..."` fallback handles the edge case where the server returns `{ ok: false }` without an error message.

options.label: Use optional chaining: `setDiscount(data.discount?.percent)`
options.resultLog:
Expired-code case: Server: 200 { ok: false, error: "COUPON_EXPIRED" } data.discount is undefined; data.discount?.percent is undefined; setDiscount(undefined). Component renders: nothing — the success `

` is hidden because discount is falsy. The error region is also empty. The user sees the button stop loading and... nothing. No crash, but the failure is invisible. User has no idea the coupon was rejected.
options.rationale:
Optional chaining silences the **crash** but leaves the user with **no feedback**. The success `

` doesn't render (discount is falsy). The error `

` doesn't render (error state was never set). The user clicked Apply, the button became unresponsive briefly, and then nothing visible changed. From the user's perspective the app is broken. The lesson here isn't 'fix the crash'; it's **make the error state visible**. Optional chaining is a tool for genuinely-optional fields, not for hiding bugs in the data flow.

options.label: Wrap the body parsing in try/catch — let exceptions become the error path
options.resultLog:
Expired-code case: Server: 200 { ok: false, error: "COUPON_EXPIRED" } res.ok passes; data is parsed; data.discount.percent throws TypeError. Catch fires, error state is set to "Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'percent')". Component renders:

Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'percent')

The error message is a JavaScript stack-trace fragment, not a meaningful coupon error. The server told us COUPON_EXPIRED; the user sees a parser-internal error message that makes no sense to them.
options.rationale: Try/catch catches the TypeError that the property access throws — so the app doesn't crash — but the message shown to the user is a **runtime exception string**, not the business-logic error the API sent. The server explicitly told the frontend `"error": "COUPON_EXPIRED"`; the catch path discards that meaningful information and replaces it with a JavaScript internals message. Try/catch handles *exceptions*; it doesn't handle *semantic outcomes*. The right check is on `data.ok` and `data.error`, after parsing, before reading business fields.

hints: What does the response body look like when the coupon is rejected? Does the status code tell you about business-logic failures, or does it only tell you about transport?
After `const data = await res.json()`, check `if (!data.ok) { setError(data.error); return; }` before reading `data.discount`.
explanationMd:
### Why the bug occurs
HTTP status codes are designed to communicate **transport-level outcomes** — the request reached the server (200 OK, 201 Created), the request was malformed (400 Bad Request), the resource doesn't exist (404 Not Found), the server crashed (500 Internal Server Error). Many APIs reserve HTTP statuses for these transport outcomes and put **business-logic outcomes** in the response body: was the operation valid for this user, was the input semantically correct, did the resource allow the change.

This design has real upsides — middleware (gateways, CDNs, monitoring) can treat 200 as 'request handled' without inspecting business semantics, and the body schema can carry richer error information than a status code can. The trade-off is that the frontend has to check **both layers**:
1. **HTTP status** — `res.ok` filters out transport failures.
2. **Payload shape** — `data.ok` (or the equivalent the API documents) filters out semantic rejections.

The bug in this mission is checking only layer 1 and skipping layer 2. Status is 200, frontend assumes success, reads a field that doesn't exist on rejection payloads, crashes.

### Why the fix is correct
After `res.json()` resolves, the next check is `data.ok`. Only on `data.ok === true` is it safe to read business fields like `data.discount.percent`. On `data.ok === false`, the server's error message is surfaced to the user via `setError(data.error)`. Both code paths return cleanly; the success and error UI regions render the expected output for each.

The `data.error ?? "Coupon could not be applied"` fallback handles the edge case where the server sends `{ ok: false }` without a message. A graceful default beats showing the user `undefined`.

### Status-only vs payload-only vs both
- **Status-only** (this bug): misses semantic errors. APIs that return 200 with `{ ok: false }` will crash downstream code that expects success.
- **Payload-only**: ignores transport errors. A 500 from a crashed server is just as fatal as a semantic rejection, but skipping `res.ok` means a 500 with an HTML error body crashes inside `res.json()` (HTML can't be parsed as JSON), and the catch handler shows a SyntaxError instead of the real problem.
- **Both**: the correct shape. Status check filters out network/transport, then the payload check filters out business logic.

### Why some APIs are designed this way
The alternative — using HTTP status for everything, including business outcomes — has its own problems:
- **422 Unprocessable Entity** for a rejected coupon code? Some APIs do this, but it conflates 'the request was malformed' with 'the request was valid but the result is no'.
- **403 Forbidden** for a permission issue is reasonable; but **403 for a coupon not applying** is awkward.
- **GraphQL** explicitly puts errors in the response body (the `errors` array) while keeping HTTP 200 for all valid GraphQL requests, regardless of resolver failures.
- **JSON-RPC** does the same — every response is HTTP 200 unless transport failed, and the body carries the success/error distinction.

This is a real design pattern; learn to recognise and handle it.

### Variations
Different APIs use different keys for the success flag:
- `{ ok: true | false, ... }` — the common convention in this lesson.
- `{ success: true | false, ... }` — the same idea with different naming.
- `{ status: "success" | "error", ... }` — string-tagged.
- `{ errors: [...] }` — GraphQL-style; success when `errors` is empty or absent.
- `{ error: { code, message } | null, data }` — error and data as siblings.

The pattern is the same — check the payload's success indicator before reading data fields — but the **specific key name comes from the API's documented contract**, not from a universal convention.
recruiterReview: Correct fix and you named the right concept — status and payload are two different layers. The optional-chaining distractor is the one that silences the crash and silences the bug; the user clicks the button and nothing happens, no feedback at all, which is worse than the crash because it looks like the app is broken with no path to recovery. The try/catch distractor catches the TypeError but throws away the server's meaningful error message and shows a JavaScript internals string instead. One nuance for production: when designing your own APIs, decide up front whether business errors live in HTTP status, in the body, or both — and document it. The hybrid shapes (200 for success, 4xx for business errors, 5xx for transport) work fine but have to be documented so clients know which one will fire when. Approved. ✅

tutorial.bodyMd:
## HTTP Status vs Payload Shape — Two Layers, Two Checks

When a frontend sends a request to a backend, the response can fail at **two different layers**:
1. **Transport layer** — did the request even reach the server, and did the server understand it?
2. **Business layer** — was the request semantically valid? Did the user have permission? Did the resource accept the change?

The HTTP status code communicates layer 1. The response body communicates layer 2. Many APIs use both. A frontend that checks only one of them will miss the failures expressed by the other.

### Concrete examples

**Stripe**: 200 OK with `{ "error": { "type": "card_error", "code": "card_declined" } }`. The transport succeeded; the card was declined. Status check passes; payload check fails.

**GraphQL over HTTP**: every valid GraphQL request returns 200, even when resolvers throw. The `errors` array in the body carries the failures. Status-only checks see success everywhere; payload check is mandatory.

**Many internal APIs**: `{ ok: true, data: ... }` on success, `{ ok: false, error: "..." }` on rejection. HTTP 200 for both. The `ok` flag in the body is the contract.

**Older REST APIs**: business errors as 422 Unprocessable Entity or 400 Bad Request. The status check catches the failure; the body carries the message. Payload-only doesn't notice the failure.

**Crashed backend**: 500 Internal Server Error with an HTML error page. `res.json()` throws on `<!DOCTYPE`. Payload check never runs. Status check is the only thing that catches it.

No single check is sufficient. Both layers, in order:
1. Status (`res.ok`)
2. Payload (`data.ok` or the equivalent the API documents)

### A general-purpose fetch wrapper
For any codebase making non-trivial fetches, a small wrapper makes the two-layer check explicit:

```js
async function apiCall(url, options) {
  const res = await fetch(url, options);
  if (!res.ok) {
    // Transport-level failure.
    throw new Error(`HTTP ${res.status} ${res.statusText}`);
  }
  const data = await res.json();
  if (!data.ok) {
    // Business-level rejection. The server told us the operation failed.
    throw new Error(data.error ?? "Operation failed");
  }
  return data;
}
```

Now every caller of `apiCall` gets uniform error handling, and both layers are visible. The thrown errors carry meaningful messages.

### Reading the API contract
The specific key for the success flag and error message comes from the API's documentation. Common variations:
- `{ ok: true/false }`
- `{ success: true/false }`
- `{ status: "success"/"error" }`
- `{ errors: [...] }` (presence of errors means failure)
- `{ error: null }` on success vs `{ error: { code, message } }` on failure

When integrating with a new API, the first task is **read the docs to find the contract**. If the docs don't say, log a few error responses to see the shape — and then write down what you observed so the next engineer doesn't have to.

### When the API is yours
Designing your own API gives you the choice. Common patterns:
- **Strict REST**: use HTTP status for everything. 200 for success, 4xx for client errors (including business rejections), 5xx for server errors. Body carries detail. Pros: aligns with HTTP semantics; many tools understand it. Cons: 422 vs 400 vs 403 distinctions can get fuzzy; cache layers and CDNs may treat all 4xx differently than you want.
- **Hybrid**: 200/201 for success, 4xx for malformed requests, 5xx for crashes, and `{ ok: false, error }` in 200 responses for business rejections. Pros: easy to reason about; clear separation. Cons: clients have to check both layers.
- **Always-200**: GraphQL-style. Transport errors only. Pros: simplifies caching and middleware. Cons: less HTTP-native; harder to debug with curl alone.

There's no single correct choice; pick one and document it. The bug class this mission demonstrates is **mismatched expectations** between API author and API consumer; clear documentation is the cure.

### What this means for fetch handlers
The one-line takeaway:
```js
const res = await fetch(url, opts);
if (!res.ok) throw new Error(/* transport */);
const data = await res.json();
if (!data.ok) throw new Error(/* business */);
// only here is it safe to read data fields
```
The order matters: status first (cheap, no parsing required), then payload (requires parsing the body). Reading `data.discount.percent` before either check is the bug.

### Common variations of this bug
- Reading `data.foo.bar` after only `res.ok`; semantic rejections crash on the property access.
- Reading `data.foo` after only `data.ok` and assuming a transport-error body has the same shape; an HTML 404 page can't be parsed as JSON and crashes inside `res.json()`.
- Showing the JavaScript exception message to the user instead of the server's meaningful error.
- Catching the error silently with `.catch(() => {})` and giving the user no feedback.
- Using `try/catch` as the only error path and relying on TypeErrors to indicate semantic failures.

### You just learned
HTTP status and response payload are two different layers. The frontend has to check both: status first (`res.ok` filters transport), then payload (`data.ok` or the documented equivalent filters business logic). Business fields like `data.discount.percent` are safe to read only after both checks pass. Optional chaining and try/catch silence the symptoms; the right fix is the two-layer check. Read the API's docs to learn the success-flag convention; document it clearly when designing your own.
